section.title: Data and privacy
section.intro: Free-tier AI tools generally use inputs for training. Paid and enterprise tiers generally don't. The specifics vary by tool and tier.

resources.title: OpenAI Enterprise Privacy
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.description: Data handling at each OpenAI tier. Enterprise and team privacy commitments are stronger than the free tier.

resources.title: Anthropic (Claude) Privacy Practices
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.description: Claude's data practices by tier. Pro and Team tiers do not use inputs for training. Free tier has more limited privacy protections.

resources.title: IAPP AI Governance Resource Center
resources.author: International Association of Privacy Professionals
resources.description: AI governance and privacy: GDPR implications, data protection impact assessments, cross-border data transfer.

resources.title: EU Data Protection & GDPR
resources.author: European Commission
resources.description: GDPR applies to any organization processing data of European residents, regardless of where the organization is based. AI tools processing personal data must comply.

resources.title: AI Policy Template for Nonprofits
resources.author: Community IT
resources.description: Data classification framework for nonprofits: what data can enter AI tools, what cannot, and how to make the distinction operational.

resources.title: AI Consent Guide: IRB Language for AI-Involved Research
resources.author: Rutgers University
resources.description: Consent language templates for research involving AI tools. Sample language for informing participants about AI processing.

resources.title: Informed Consent, Redefined
resources.author: Harvard Petrie-Flom Center
resources.description: Traditional consent assumes specific, bounded data uses. AI processing is neither – data can enter training sets permanently.

resources.title: SACHRP Recommendations on AI and Human Subjects Protections
resources.author: OHRP/SACHRP
resources.description: Advisory committee recommendations on how AI intersects with IRB oversight and human subjects protections.
resources.note: SACHRP was terminated in March 2025 as part of HHS restructuring (82,000 to 62,000 staff). The recommendations remain substantively relevant, but the institutional mechanism for updating federal AI guidance in research is gone.

resources.title: HHS Cuts Impacting Human Subject Research – No More SACHRP?
resources.author: Holland & Knight
resources.description: Legal analysis of what the elimination of SACHRP means for human research protections in the US.

resources.title: Consent for Secondary Use of Data in AI Research
resources.author: BMC Medical Ethics
resources.description: Once data enters a training set, consent is effectively irrevocable – data shapes all future predictions. No established mechanism for withdrawal.

resources.title: AI and Data Privacy: A Primer
resources.author: Future of Privacy Forum
resources.description: How AI systems handle data: training vs. inference, data retention, and what privacy commitments actually mean technically.

warningCallout.title: Bright line
warningCallout.body: Participant data, patient information, unpublished research findings, personnel documents, legal communications, and anything covered by an IRB protocol should not enter any AI tool regardless of tier. The question is whether the original informed consent covers this use.
